focus: correctness: logic correctness: decay/fuzzy scoring, query edges, jsonl round-state

title: Review FILE coverage gaps use the wrong edge direction
detail: findCoverageGaps() includes FILE nodes in the review target set, but then treats every review target as covered only by outgoing COVERS or EVIDENCE_FOR edges from the node. The review seed path emits SLICE -> FILE COVERS edges, and hotspot/scoring code also treats files as covered when the file is the edge target, so query.cjs --query-type coverage_gaps can report covered files as gaps.
claim: A normal review graph with a seeded SLICE/DIMENSION -> FILE COVERS edge can still report that FILE as uncovered because findCoverageGaps checks e.source_id = n.id for FILE nodes.
counterevidenceSought: Checked the review seed transform, convergence scoring, hotspot saturation, and query.cjs exposure. I did not find a producer that converts seeded file coverage into outgoing FILE edges.
alternativeExplanation: If FILE nodes are intended to be producers rather than covered targets, the seed and scoring paths are the inconsistent side; either way the live query/scoring contract is split.
downgradeTrigger: Downgrade if the review graph contract is amended to state that FILE coverage gaps intentionally require outgoing FILE -> evidence/finding edges and seeded SLICE -> FILE edges are not meant to satisfy coverage_gaps.
suggestedFix: Split review gap logic by kind. Treat FILE nodes as covered by inbound COVERS from a slice/dimension and/or inbound IN_FILE, then add an integration test with a SLICE/DIMENSION -> FILE edge that must not produce a FILE gap.

title: Research claim verification query disagrees with convergence scoring
detail: computeResearchClaimVerificationRateFromData() counts any present verification_status other than unresolved as verified, but findUnverifiedClaims() only accepts literal verified. Claims with statuses like supported or confirmed can pass the convergence score while still appearing in unverified_claims query output and warning blockers.
claim: The query and signal paths use different verified-claim predicates, so the same CLAIM row can be both verified for claimVerificationRate and unverified for findUnverifiedClaims.
counterevidenceSought: Searched for a documented finite status enum or adapter that normalizes all verified statuses to literal verified before query time; found only the signal-side contract that non-unresolved is verified.
alternativeExplanation: If only verified and unresolved are valid statuses in practice, this is latent until another accepted status appears, but the signal code explicitly accepts the broader contract.
downgradeTrigger: Downgrade if producers are constrained by schema/tests to emit only verification_status values verified or unresolved and the signal comment is corrected to that narrower enum.
suggestedFix: Extract one shared isClaimVerified predicate and use it in both query and signal code. Add a test for a non-unresolved status across claimVerificationRate and unverified_claims.

title: Round-state append corrupts a valid final JSONL line without a newline
detail: repairRoundStateJsonl() treats a trailing valid JSON object without a newline as clean. appendRoundStateRecord() then appends the next JSON object immediately after it, producing a concatenated invalid line; the next repair pass can truncate back to the prior newline and drop records.
claim: A state file ending with a valid JSON record but no trailing newline is considered unrepaired, and the next append corrupts the JSONL stream.
counterevidenceSought: Checked round-state tests; they cover clean files with a trailing newline and invalid partial tails, but not a valid no-newline tail. The append code does not add a separator based on current file content.
alternativeExplanation: If the state writer contract guarantees every durable record always includes the newline, this is a recovery-edge bug rather than steady-state corruption; crash/manual recovery can still leave this exact tail shape.
downgradeTrigger: Downgrade if a lower layer proves append writes and crash recovery can never leave a complete JSON object without its trailing newline.
suggestedFix: Under the append lock, ensure a non-empty file ends with newline before appending, or make repair normalize a valid no-newline tail by preserving it and adding the separator. Add a two-record regression for this case.

title: Read-side JSONL repair ignores the writer lock
detail: appendRoundStateRecord() acquires a lock before repair/write, but readRoundStateRecords() repairs by default without acquiring or checking that lock. A concurrent reader can see a partial trailing line and truncate the state file while the writer still owns the cooperative lock.
claim: The read path can mutate round-state JSONL without participating in the single-writer lock protocol, creating a data-loss race with active appends.
counterevidenceSought: Looked for read-side lock acquisition or a caller guarantee that readRoundStateRecords() is only invoked after writers finish; the module default remains repair-on-read and the exported reader has no lock path.
alternativeExplanation: If all current readers run strictly after orchestration completion, the race is latent until resume/monitoring overlaps with a write. The module API itself does not enforce that ordering.
downgradeTrigger: Downgrade if all production callers are proven single-process and non-overlapping, or if regular-file append visibility is otherwise wrapped by an external process lock.
suggestedFix: Do not truncate from readRoundStateRecords() unless the reader acquires the same lock, or make repair false by default for reads and expose a separate locked repair operation for resume recovery.

title: Fuzzy substring scoring lets very short aliases match at the default merge threshold
detail: calculateNameSimilarity() returns 0.85 for any substring match, and the default consolidation threshold is also 0.85. Because this branch runs before the fuzzy-word minimum length gate, very short same-kind names can cluster with unrelated names that merely contain those letters.
claim: Short substring aliases can produce default-threshold consolidation candidates even when the token-level fuzzy guard would have rejected them.
counterevidenceSought: Checked fuzzy-merge tests; they cover cross-kind rejection and a normal long-phrase near duplicate, but not short aliases or substring false positives.
alternativeExplanation: If callers only query with descriptive multi-word names, this remains a robustness gap. The public API accepts arbitrary node names, including short file/symbol aliases.
downgradeTrigger: Close if a caller-side invariant rejects short query names and short candidate names before fuzzy matching.
suggestedFix: Require a minimum substring length and token boundary for substring scoring, or lower substring-only matches below the default threshold. Add tests for two-character aliases and punctuation-heavy names.
